narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 82.1% chance that you would not pass, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates for its graduates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your academic decile from Year 3 of school, which ranked quite low and significantly pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar exam failure, unlike the much higher average ranks around the 6th decile seen among students who passed. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom of the scale compared to passers' typical mid-range positions, further reinforcing the model's concern about early struggles carrying forward. Your LSAT score, while solid, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, though not enough to offset the other factors. Coming from a middle-income family and planning full-time studies aligns with many who succeed, but it didn't outweigh the academic red flags here. Compared to admitted students, who often have undergraduate GPAs around 3.24 and LSATs near 37, your 2.90 GPA and 35 LSAT placed you below those benchmarks, amplifying the impact of your deciles. These top three elements—Year 3 decile leading the pack, followed by Year 1 decile, with LSAT offering some counterbalance—were the key drivers tipping the scales against you. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your dedication to full-time law studies ahead. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted improvement in later years, perhaps by seeking tutoring or study groups proven to help students climb those ranks. Retaking the LSAT could build on its positive influence, and highlighting any upward GPA trends in reapplications might shift perceptions. Many students in similar spots have turned things around by addressing these core academic predictors head-on.
